county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Lawrenceville holds records from 1821. Lawrence County is on the Wabash River at the Indiana border — check Knox County, Indiana for related families. Oil boom in the early 1900s at the Lawrence County oil field was significant — drilling and lease records. Created from Crawford and Edwards — check both for pre-1821 records. Lawrence County Historical and Genealogical Society. IRAD at Eastern Illinois University. Vincennes-to-St. Louis trail crossed here. Early Upland South settlers.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1821. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1821. County Clerk issues licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (details improve post-1870s). Statewide marriage registration from 1962.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1821. Illinois Circuit Court handles probate matters. Estate inventories often list heirs and personal property in detail.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, naturalizations (pre-1906), and judgments from 1821. Illinois Circuit Court is the trial court of general jurisdiction. Early records include debt cases, divorce proceedings, and guardianship matters that identify family relationships.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Lawrence County Clerk, Lawrenceville / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax assessment rolls list property owners, land descriptions, and property values. County Clerk records from 1821. Early tax lists serve as census substitutes. Illinois State Archives holds some county tax records on microfilm.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some tax records at Illinois State Archives on microfilm; IRAD holdings vary by county

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Lawrence County
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Illinois. As a public-land state, original land entries were through federal land offices. Cash entry, credit, and preemption patents. Illinois State Archives also holds duplicate patent records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: BLM-GLO digitized — searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Oil and Gas Lease Records — Lawrence County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Oil and gas lease agreements recorded in county deed books. Illinois oil fields (particularly in southeastern Illinois) generated extensive lease and mineral rights records from the early 1900s onward. These records identify landowners and track property transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved in county deed records

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Dixon holds records from 1839. Dixon was Ronald Reagan's boyhood home. Lee County attracted settlers from New York and New England. Check Ogle County for pre-1839 records. Lee County Historical and Genealogical Society in Dixon. Black Hawk War (1832) — Fort Dixon was a significant military post; Abraham Lincoln served as a captain of volunteers nearby. Rock River communities. IRAD at Northern Illinois University. Strong agricultural heritage — corn and livestock. Railroad towns.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1839. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Pontiac holds records from 1837. Pontiac is the seat of Livingston County on Route 66 — the old Pontiac courthouse is a Route 66 landmark. Illinois State Penitentiary at Pontiac (1871) — prison records at Illinois State Archives. Check McLean and LaSalle counties for pre-1837 records. Livingston County Historical Society. Dwight area had notable German settlement. IRAD at Illinois State University. Strong agricultural heritage. Chatsworth train wreck (1887) killed 82 people — documented in county records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1837. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention, petitions for naturalization, and certificates. Pre-1906 records at county Circuit Court. Post-1906 copies at NARA Great Lakes Region (Chicago). Illinois attracted massive European immigration — German, Irish, Swedish, Norwegian, Czech, Polish, Italian, Lithuanian, and other groups.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Many Illinois naturalization records indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Lincoln holds records from 1839. Lincoln is the only city named for Abraham Lincoln before he became president — Lincoln christened the town in 1853. Logan County is in the heart of central Illinois. Postville Courthouse where Lincoln practiced law. Check Sangamon County for pre-1839 records. Logan County Genealogical and Historical Society. IRAD at University of Illinois Springfield. Lincoln Heritage Museum at Lincoln College. Illinois Central Railroad corridor. Agricultural heritage.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1839. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.

source_profiles.source_name: Eighth Judicial Circuit Court Records — Logan County
source_profiles.repository: Logan County Circuit Clerk, Lincoln / Illinois State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Abraham Lincoln practiced law on Illinois' Eighth Judicial Circuit from 1837 to 1860. Circuit court records from this period include cases Lincoln argued and contemporaries he worked with. Court records also document the legal, economic, and social history of central Illinois families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Circuit court records at county level; Lincoln Legal Papers project has compiled many Lincoln-related documents

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Decatur holds records from 1829. Abraham Lincoln's family moved to Macon County in 1830 — their first home in Illinois was near Decatur. Lincoln Trail Homestead State Memorial. Decatur became an important industrial city — A.E. Staley (corn processing) and Caterpillar employment records. Macon County Historical Society. Check Shelby County for pre-1829 records. IRAD at University of Illinois Springfield. Strong railroad heritage (Wabash Railroad shops). African American community in Decatur from Great Migration.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1829. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.

source_profiles.source_name: African American Church and Community Records — Macon County
source_profiles.repository: Vivian G. Harsh Research Collection (Chicago Public Library) / local churches and historical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: African American church records (AME, Baptist, COGIC), fraternal organization records, and community newspapers. The Great Migration (1910-1970) brought hundreds of thousands of African Americans from the South to Illinois, particularly Chicago and industrial cities. Chicago Defender newspaper is a critical source.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Vivian G. Harsh Collection at Chicago Public Library is the largest African American history collection in the Midwest

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Abraham Lincoln practiced law on Illinois' Eighth Judicial Circuit from 1837 to 1860. source_profiles.survival_notes: Circuit court records at county level; Lincoln Legal Papers project has compiled many Lincoln-related documents

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Carlinville holds records from 1829. Macoupin County is known for the 'Million Dollar Courthouse' in Carlinville (1870) — one of the most expensive county courthouses built in the 19th century. Coal mining heritage especially in Virden, Staunton, and Mt. Olive areas — Virden mine riot (1898) and Mt. Olive miners' cemetery (Mother Jones buried here). Created from Madison and Greene — check both for pre-1829 records. IRAD at University of Illinois Springfield. Strong labor union history.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mine inspection reports, employment records, accident reports, and company records. Illinois coal mining drew workers from Italy, Lithuania, Poland, and other European countries as well as Appalachian migrants. Annual coal reports published by the State of Illinois list mines and operators.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Mine inspection records at Illinois State Archives; some company records in local collections

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Edwardsville holds records from 1812 — one of Illinois' original counties. Madison County covers the American Bottom across from St. Louis — French colonial settlements at Cahokia preceded American settlement. Edwardsville was home to Ninian Edwards, territorial governor. Madison County Historical Society. Alton on the Mississippi was where Elijah Lovejoy, abolitionist editor, was murdered (1837). Lewis and Clark set out from Camp Dubois in Madison County (1804). IRAD at Southern Illinois University Edwardsville. Extensive early records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1812. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Illinois deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — original patents from BLM/GLO.

source_profiles.source_name: French Colonial Catholic Parish Records — Madison County
source_profiles.repository: Diocese of Belleville / Illinois State Archives / Newberry Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: French Catholic parish registers from the Illinois Country — baptisms, marriages, and burials. Cahokia (1699), Kaskaskia (1703), and Prairie du Rocher parishes have records predating American settlement. Records in French and Latin. These are among the oldest church records in the Midwest.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers well-preserved; some transcribed and published; microfilm copies at multiple repositories

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Salem holds records from 1823. Salem was the birthplace of William Jennings Bryan. Marion County was an important crossroads in southern-central Illinois. Created from Fayette and Jefferson — check both for pre-1823 records. Marion County Historical Society. Oil production from the Salem oil field (discovered 1938) generated extensive land records. IRAD at Southern Illinois University Carbondale. Bryan Bennett Library in Salem. Early Upland South settlers. Strong Methodist and Baptist church records.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Lacon holds records from 1839. Marshall County is on the Illinois River — Lacon and Henry were river towns with steamboat commerce. Check Putnam County for pre-1839 records. Marshall County Historical Society. Strong agricultural heritage — fertile Illinois River bottomlands. Henry was known as a major grain shipping point. IRAD at Illinois State University. Early settlers from New England, New York, and Upland South. Township and school records supplement county records.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Clerk in Havana holds records from 1841. Mason County is on the Illinois River — Havana was an important river port and commercial fishing center. Sand Ridge State Forest area. Abraham Lincoln practiced law in Havana courthouse. Check Tazewell and Menard counties for pre-1841 records. Mason County Genealogical and Historical Society. IRAD at University of Illinois Springfield. Kilbourne and Bath on the river were early communities. Strong agricultural and fishing heritage.
